coredata.dc:title: Both carbon dots precursor and organic bridge ligands for coordination polymers: AMP-based ratiometric fluorescent probes and its application in bovine serum albumin detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Yuwei
Shuai, Xue
Ruan, Xiangyan
Wang, Yuejiao
Wei, Yun
coredata.dc:description: Bovine serum albumin (BSA) is one of the most abundant proteins in serum, and its high-throughput detection is still one of the current challenges. Nitrogen‑phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Adenosine monophosphate (AMP) was used as a precursor for the synthesis of CDs, providing the required carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus sources for the CDs. It was also used as an organic bridge ligand for coordination polymers. Upon addition of the lanthanide metal ion Tb3+, the AMP molecules formed lanthanide coordination polymers in solution, resulting in fabrication of the novel ratiometric fluorescent probe AMP-CDs@Tb with dual emission centers. This fabricated method greatly reduced the complexity of dual-emission CDs doped with lanthanide metals. The designed ratiometric fluorescent probe only needed one precursor AMP to realize the synthesis of CDs and bound to lanthanide metal ions as an organic ligand, this probe could be used for rapid and sensitive analysis of BSA. The linear relationship was good when the concentration of BSA was 0.1 to 650 μM, and the LOD was 0.042 μM. In addition, the possible detection mechanism of BSA was explored through fluorescence lifetime and density functional theory calculations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratio fluorescent probe
Lanthanide coordination polymer
BSA
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and optical properties of AMP-CDs@Tb

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of AMP-CDs with HRTEM in the lower right inset; (B) TEM images of AMP-CDs@Tb; (C) AFM image and the height profile along the green line of AMP-CDs dispersed in aqueous solution; (D) XRD patterns of AMP-CDs; (E) Raman spectra of AMP-CDs; (F) SEM image of AMP-CDs@Tb; (G) AMP-CDs@Tb energy spectrum; (H) FT-IR spectrum of AMP-CDs@Tb.
XPS spectra of (A) AMP-CDs@Tb; (B) C 1s; (C) N 1s; (D) O 1s, and (E) Tb 3d.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of AMP-CDs at 360 nm excitation wavelength; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Tb3+ at 290 nm excitation wavelength; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of AMP-CDs@Tb at 290 nm excitation wavelength; (D) Scheme of AMP as CDs precursor and organic bridge ligands for coordination polymers.
(A) Variation of F420/F545 ratio at different pH; (B) Variation of F420/F545 ratio at different salt concentrations; (C) Variation curve of F420/F545 ratio at 40 min of UV irradiation.
(A) Zeta potential of AMP-CDs, AMP-CDs@Tb, and AMP-CDs@Tb + BSA; (B) Selectivity of AMP-CDs@Tb detection of BSA; (C) Linearity of AMP-CDs@Tb detection of BSA.

(A) FL decay traces curve; (B) Scheme of possible bursting mechanisms; (C) Major amino acid structures in BSA; (D) The optimized structures of AMP-CDs and the corresponding energy gap (eV); (E) UV vis absorption spectra and FL excitation and emission spectra of AMP-CDs; (F) UV vis absorption spectra and FL excitation and emission spectra of AMP-CDs@Tb; (G) UV–vis spectra of AMP-CDs, Tb3+, AMP-CDs@Tb, BSA, and AMP-CDs@Tb + BSA.
Detection of BSA in bovine serum 1 samples.
Detection of BSA in bovine serum 2 samples.
Detection of BSA in bovine serum 3 samples.

head.ce:title: Both carbon dots precursor and organic bridge ligands for coordination polymers: AMP-based ratiometric fluorescent probes and its application in bovine serum albumin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Obstetrics and Gynecology Hospital, Capital Medical University. Beijing Maternal and Child Health Care Hospital, Beijing 100026, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Capital Medical University. Beijing Maternal and Child Health Care Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Obstetrics and Gynecology Hospital, Capital Medical University. Beijing Maternal and Child Health Care Hospital, Beijing 100026, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bovine serum albumin (BSA) is one of the most abundant proteins in serum, and its high-throughput detection is still one of the current challenges. Nitrogen‑phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Adenosine monophosphate (AMP) was used as a precursor for the synthesis of CDs, providing the required carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus sources for the CDs. It was also used as an organic bridge ligand for coordination polymers. Upon addition of the lanthanide metal ion Tb3+, the AMP molecules formed lanthanide coordination polymers in solution, resulting in fabrication of the novel ratiometric fluorescent probe AMP-CDs@Tb with dual emission centers. This fabricated method greatly reduced the complexity of dual-emission CDs doped with lanthanide metals. The designed ratiometric fluorescent probe only needed one precursor AMP to realize the synthesis of CDs and bound to lanthanide metal ions as an organic ligand, this probe could be used for rapid and sensitive analysis of BSA. The linear relationship was good when the concentration of BSA was 0.1 to 650 μM, and the LOD was 0.042 μM. In addition, the possible detection mechanism of BSA was explored through fluorescence lifetime and density functional theory calculations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, the most lethal disease in the world, has been the focus of research in various countries for a long time [1]. Although scientists had invested considerable effort, its cure rate was still unsatisfactory [2–4]. Early diagnosis by using detection of cancer markers of protein is one of the important factors to improve the cure rate of cancer [5,6]. For example, carcinoembryonic antigen in body fluids could be detected for the prediction, diagnosis, and postoperative monitoring of tumors [7]. Serum albumin (SA) was one of the most abundant and critical globular proteins in the blood of mammals, which not only maintained the osmotic pressure of plasma in the body, but also ensured the health of the organism's major organs (liver and kidneys, etc.) [8–11]. Abnormalities in the gastrointestinal tract and kidney function lead to high SA levels, and liver abnormalities or malnutrition lead to low SA levels [12–14]. Therefore, the level of SA was an important indicator of the health status of the organism [15–19]. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) as an example, it is a prominent protein in bovine serum, and widely used in biochemical experiments [20–25]. Humans would be exposed to BSA by eating beef. Once exposed to a certain amount of BSA, they would suffer from inflammation, insulin-dependent diabetes, mad cow disease, and other diseases [26]. Therefore, the detection of BSA had attracted increasing attention in immunology and bioanalytical research [27]. Current methods for the detection of BSA included electrochemical methods, mass spectrometry, electrophoresis, and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [28–34]. However, these methods were still limited to high-throughput protein analysis due to expensive instruments and time-consuming and cumbersome processes. Therefore, the simpler, more efficient, and accurate fluorescent probe method was now becoming the most desirable detection method and was gaining more and more attention [35–44].

Carbon dots (CDs) were a novel type of fluorescent probe nanomaterials. Since the accidental discovery of CDs by Xu et al. in 2004, they had attracted much attention due to their excellent properties [45]. CDs had the characteristics of good solubility, high quantum yield, and resistance to photobleaching [46], So CDs had been used in sensing [47], imaging [48], nanomedicine [49], and photocatalysis [50] by many researchers around the world. For example, nitrogen-doped CDs were used as fluorescent probes to construct a fluorescent biosensor based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect for the detection of metallothioneins [51]. However, using a single fluorescence emission as the signal in these systems was susceptible to interference from multiple factors, including variations in the intensity of the excitation light source, environmental conditions, and probe concentration. These interferences could lead to reduce stability and reliability of the detection system. Therefore, dual-emitting CDs had been reported, which was a type of fluorescent nanomaterials with unique optical properties and functional diversity. Compared with traditional single-emitting CDs, dual-emitting CDs could produce two different wavelengths of fluorescence emission at the same excitation wavelength and had unique spectral resolution capabilities [52]. This characteristic gave it an excellent advantage in ratiometric fluorescence sensing, because the ratio signal could effectively reduce the interference of environmental factors (such as probe concentration, excitation light intensity, etc.) on the detection results, significantly improved detection sensitivity and accuracy. In addition, dual-emitting CDs usually achieved their multi-color emission characteristics by regulating their surface functional groups, precursors or doping elements, and had excellent tunability and broad application potential [53].

Heteroatom doping was an important way to improve the fluorescence properties of CDs. The nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using adenosine monophosphate (AMP) as raw material. The base nitrogen/oxygen atoms, pentose oxygen atoms, and phosphate groups of AMP were all potential binding sites for metal ions [54], would coordinate with terbium ions (Tb3+) to form a polymer network [55]. However, additional ligands were often required for CDs to bind to the lanthanide metals. In this work, Tb3+ was mixed into AMP-CDs prepared with AMP as a precursor. AMP served as an organic bridging ligand and self-assemble with Tb3+ to form a polymer network. This unique CDs coupled lanthanide coordination polymer was recorded as AMP-CDs@Tb. The ratiometric fluorescent probe constructed in this way simultaneously solved the CDs synthetic precursor and the organic bridging ligand in the polymer network structure through a single reagent. On one hand, AMP served as the precursor of CDs, and the CDs formed through the carbonization process not only inherited the chromophore characteristics of its molecules and obtained good water solubility and fluorescence properties. On the other hand, the phosphate group and amino group in the AMP molecule could form a stable coordination structure with Tb3+, acting as a connecting bridging agent, allowing CDs and Tb3+ to form a composite system at the nanoscale. This design not only gave the material unique dual-emission characteristics, but also significantly enhanced the Tb3+ luminescence efficiency through energy transfer between Tb3+ and CDs, while achieving diversification and controllability of material properties. The dual role of AMP in such composite materials provided new ideas for the development of fluorescent probes, especially in the fields of ratiometric fluorescence sensing and multi-functional detection, which greatly reduced the tedious synthesis. In addition, the ratiometric fluorescence method achieved highly sensitive detection of BSA, which greatly reduced the cost of detection. Finally, the structure-activity relationship of AMP-CDs@Tb was explored to reveal its binding mechanism with BSA.

Adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and terbium nitrate (TbNO3) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) was purchased from Beijing Huateng Chemical Co., Ltd.; Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd.; Sodium chloride (NaCl) was purchased from Shanghai Beide Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Cytochrome C (Cyt-c), Pepsin (Pep), Human hemoglobin (Hb), Trypsin (Try), Albumin-Egg (Alb), Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), Lysozyme (Lys), Bovine hemoglobin (Hem) and myoglobin (Myo) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All experimental water was ultrapure water.

The final samples were characterized using several analytical techniques. X-ray diffraction (XRD, X'Pert PRO MPD) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) was employed to examine the crystal structure, with a scanning angle (2θ) ranged from 10° to 80°. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200) was used to observe the microstructure of the products. Surface functional groups were analyzed via Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS20). Raman spectroscopy (LabRam HR Evolution, Horiba Scientific; Renishaw inVia; Thermo Fischer DXR) was utilized to investigate the phases of the samples. Elemental composition and surface chemistry were assessed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo K-Alpha). The AFM photo was from Bruker Dimension ICON atomic force microscope. The SEM photo was from S-4700 scanning electron microscope.

A mount of 165 mg AMP was dissolved in 25 mL deionized water, ultrasounded and placed in a high-pressure reactor using hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 12 h and then cooled to room temperature. Impurities were removed by filtration with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, dialysis was performed with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h, and the dialyzed CDs solution mixed with Tb3+ (30 mM) solution and shaken at room temperature for 30 min to obtain AMP-CDs@Tb. Then it was kept in a 4 °C refrigerator for use.

The BSA solution (100 μL, 0.10 μM) and Tris-HCl buffer (100 μL, 0.1 M, pH = 7.4) were added to AMP-CDs@Tb (25 μL), and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min in an oscillator. The fluorescence emission spectrum was measured at an excitation wavelength of 290 nm. The best detection conditions were shown in Fig. S1 of the optimization experiment.

The quantum yield (QY) of AMP-CDs and AMP-CDs@Tb was determined according to the method reported in the reference [56]. The UV absorbance and fluorescence spectra of AMP-CDs, AMP-CDs@Tb and quinine sulfate were measured using a standard solution of quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference (QYquinine sulfate = 54 %).

The quantum yields (Φ
x ) of AMP-CDs and AMP-CDs@Tb are obtained by the formula:
Φ x
=
Φ Q
×
I x
I Q
×
A Q
A x
×
η x 2
η Q 2

Φ
Q is the quantum yield of quinine sulfate (usually 0.54 in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution). I
x and I
Q are the fluorescence integrated intensities of the sample and quinine sulfate, respectively. A
x and A
Q are the absorbances of the sample and quinine sulfate at the excitation wavelength, respectively. η
x and η
Q are the refractive indices of the sample and quinine sulfate solvents, respectively (if the solvents are the same, the refractive index effect can be ignored).

According to the UV and fluorescence spectra of quinine sulfate in Fig. S2 of the Supporting Information, the quantum yield of AMP-CDs was 59.04 % and that of AMP-CDs@Tb was 85.21 % by calculation. For materials with multiple fluorescence emission peaks, it is necessary to calculate the quantum yield (
Φ 1
,
Φ 2
…
Φ n
) of each peak separately and add them together to obtain the total quantum yield
Φ Total
. (
Φ Total
=
Φ 1
+
Φ 2
+ … +
Φ n
).

The bovine blood without anticoagulant was collected in a centrifuge tube and stand at room temperature for 1 h to coagulate naturally. The coagulated blood sample was placed in a 4 °C refrigerator for 2 h to promote the separation of serum. Then it was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant was collected after centrifugation, and stored in a −20 °C refrigerator.

The TEM image of AMP-CDs was shown in Fig. 1 (A). The AMP-CDs had a spherical structure with a uniform size distribution and good dispersion in aqueous solution, their particle sizes ranged from 7.83 to 14.76 nm (the average size of 8.10 nm). The inset in the lower right corner was a high-resolution TEM image of the CDs, and the lattice spacing of the CDs was measured to be 0.35 nm, corresponding to the (102) diffraction plane of graphite, indicating that the CDs had a certain graphitized structure. Fig. 1 (B) showed the TEM of AMP-CDs@Tb. After doping with Tb3+, the AMP-CDs still existed and showed obvious reticular cross-linking morphology, which could prove that Tb3+ had been successfully doped into the CDs.

The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images along the green line, as shown in Fig. 1 (C), confirmed that the thickness of the CDs ranged from 1 nm to 2.5 nm. The elemental composition of AMP-CDs@Tb was further explored by XRD. The XRD pattern of the AMP-CDs revealed a peak at 2θ = 29.8°, which was attributed to the graphite structure with a layer spacing (002) of 0.45 nm. This increase in layer spacing, compared to the 0.335 nm observed in graphite might have been due to steric hindrance of functional groups on the planar edges of sp2 carbon or planar deformation of sp3 carbon in the sp2 carbon domains [57]. As shown in Fig. 1 (D) for the XRD image, there was a small peak at 2θ at 41.8°, which was caused by the d spacing of graphene (100). This value was not much different from the lattice edge spacing observed in the TEM image. The Raman spectra of AMP-CDs showed two peaks at 1374 and 1617 cm−1 corresponding to disordered carbon structure (D-band) and graphitized carbon structure (G-band), suggesting the presence of both sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbons in the AMP-CDs. The ratio of I D/I G was 0.73, indicating that the CDs had a highly graphitized structure, as shown in Fig. 1 (E).

As shown by SEM Fig. 1 (F), AMP-CDs@Tb was in amorphous form. The atomic percentages of C, N, O, P, and Tb were 10.2 %, 21.7 %, 63.7 %, 0.3 %, and 4.1 % as shown in the EDS energy spectrum of Fig. 1 (G). The surface structure of AMP-CDs@Tb was probed by FT-IR (Fig. 1 (H)). The broad absorption peak located at 3391 cm−1 was the OH stretching vibration peak, and the peaks at 1634 and 1486 cm−1 were attributed to the CO and CN stretching vibration peaks. The absorption peaks observed between 500 cm−1 and 900 cm−1 were related to the bending vibrations of CH in the CDs. Additionally, the peak at 1084 cm−1 belonged to the PO bond, indicating that P was successfully doped into the CDs [58].

From the full XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 (A)), there were four peaks of AMP-CDs@Tb with binding energies of 284.3, 407.0, 532.5, and 1276.9 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Tb 3d, and the corresponding atomic percentages were of 65.4 %, 7.4 %, 26.4 %, and 0.9 %. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2 (B)) showed three peaks of 288.3, 285.7, and 284.3 eV, corresponding to CO/CN, CO, and CC bonds, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s indicated the presence of CN (407.1 eV) and CN (399.8 eV) bonds in the AMP-CDs@Tb (Fig. 2 (C)). The XPS high-resolution spectra of O 1s could be characterized by two peaks of 532.9 eV and 531.5 eV, and the presence of CO and CO bonds was hypothesized (Fig. 2 (D)). The high-resolution spectrum of Tb 3d indicated that Tb3+ existed in two states, 1276.8 and 1243.3 eV corresponding to the 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 states of Tb, respectively (Fig. 2 (E)) [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optical properties of AMP-CDs@Tb
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of AMP-CDs from adenylates by hydrothermal method was simple, rapid, and could be prepared in bulk. AMP decomposed at high temperatures and subsequently the decomposition products polymerized and began to form dots of disordered structure at high temperatures. These dots grew by successive carbonization at high temperatures and eventually became crystalline. The CDs formed under different hydrothermal conditions had different optical properties, so the amount of AMP added and the hydrothermal time and temperature during the reaction were optimized. As shown in Fig. S3, the optimal reaction conditions for the synthesis of adenylate-derived CDs by the hydrothermal method were 20 mM AMP, 10 h reaction time, and 200 °C high temperature and high-pressure environment. Adenylate contained nitrogen and phosphorus elements, and the high fluorescence performance exhibited by AMP-CDs was related to the nitrogen and phosphorus elements introduced by bases as well as phosphate groups.

The fluorescence emission spectra of AMP-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (330–380 nm) exhibited unique optical properties (Fig. S4 (A)). The AMP-CDs exhibited excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior, with fluorescence emission peaks around 420 nm and the highest fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The 10.0 mL of AMP-CDs solution was mixed with 10.0 mL of terbium nitrate solution of different concentrations and shaken at room temperature for 30 min. As shown in Fig. S4 (B), when the Tb3+ concentration was 40 mM, the ratio fluorescence signal of the probe was the most obvious. This was because the small amount of AMP contained in the AMP-CDs solution self-assembled with Tb3+ to form AMP/Tb polymers and wrapped AMP-CDs in the formed polymer network in the solution. This unique CDs-coupled lanthanide coordination polymer was referred as AMP-CDs@Tb. The fluorescence spectra of AMP-CDs, Tb3+, and AMP-CDs@Tb was shown in Fig. 3 (A–C), respectively. (The insets in Fig. 3 (A) and (C) were actual photographs of the probe taken under 365 nm UV light) The as-prepared AMP-CDs@Tb had characteristic peaks of AMP and Tb3+ at the same time, which proved that the dual-emission fluorescent CDs material had been successfully prepared. The composite material synthesized using AMP could be used as both a CDs precursor and an organic bridge ligands at the same time, as shown in the scheme in Fig. 3 (D).

In order to avoid the unwanted influence of the octave peak on the subsequent detection, the AMP-CDs@Tb was investigated using filters, as shown in Fig. S5, at the optimal excitation wavelength of AMP-CDs@Tb of 290 nm with H2O it was found that in the absence of filters, a strong octave peak occurred at 580 nm. When different wavelengths of the filters (300 to 650 nm) were used, it was found that the frequency doubling peak completely disappeared after the filter wavelength exceeded 400 nm, so the 300 to 450 nm wavelength filter was chosen to test AMP-CDs@Tb. The effect of the frequency doubling peak could have been eliminated at 400 nm, so it was assumed that the peaks at 545 nm and 605 nm of AMP-CDs@Tb was the characteristic peaks of Tb3+, not the frequency doubling peaks. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of Tb3+ were not changed by the influence of the octave peaks, so the fluorescence at 545 nm and 420 nm were used as the proportional fluorescence for the target detection in all the subsequent experiments.

After the purified CDs was obtained, the effect of the pH of the buffer solution on AMP-CDs@Tb was tested at the excitation wavelength of 290 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 (A), in the pH range of 4–10, the overall trend of F420/F545 values of AMP-CDs@Tb was gradually decreased with the gradual increase of pH, but since bovine serum was tested as the actual sample, and the pH range of bovine serum was in the range of 7.35–7.45, the optimal pH value using Tris-HCl buffer solution because its pH was close to that of bovine blood. As a fluorescent sensing material, AMP-CDs@Tb was required to have stable fluorescence performance in different environments in practical applications, so the salt resistance and photobleaching resistance of AMP-CDs@Tb was explored. The concentration of salt in the solution might influence the surface groups of AMP-CDs@Tb, which in turn affected the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs@Tb. Fig. 4 (B) showed the F420/F545 values of AMP-CDs@Tb at different NaCl concentrations (0 to 500 mM). Although the ratiometric fluorescence values of AMP-CDs@Tb at different NaCl concentrations varied, overall AMP-CDs@Tb was more tolerant to salt without significant changes. Photobleaching was a phenomenon in which the fluorescent intensity of a fluorescent substance gradually diminishes or even disappeared under continuous irradiation of excitation light. Fig. 4 (C) showed the fluorescence intensity change values of the characteristic peaks at 420 and 545 nm of AMP-CDs@Tb under continuous irradiation with a UV lamp for 1 h. From the figure, both basically remained unchanged, indicating that AMP-CDs@Tb had good resistance to photobleaching.

The ability of AMP-CDs to bind to BSA was firstly assayed by Zeta potential. As shown in Fig. 5 (A), AMP-CDs were negatively charged at −18.72 mV due to the many hydroxyl groups on its surface like most CDs. When AMP-CDs were mixed with Tb3+, the Zeta potential was increased to 5.90 mV, demonstrating that the CDs system contained a large amount of positively charged Tb3+, indicating that the synthesis of AMP-CDs@Tb were successfully synthesized. Finally, after the addition of the target BSA, the electronegativity in the system continued to increase to 14.20 mV due to the positive charge of BSA.

To investigate the selectivity of AMP-CDs@Tb for BSA, nine proteins, including BSA, bovine hemoglobin (Hem), human hemoglobin (Hb), cytochrome C (Cyt-c), myoglobin (Myo), pepsin (Pep), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), trypsin (Try), and lysozyme (Lys), were respectively interacted with AMP-CDs@Tb under the optimal experimental conditions. The fluorescence spectra of each protein were shown in Fig. S6. Among the ratiometric fluorescence values of the nine different proteins detected by AMP-CDs@Tb, only BSA had the largest ratiometric fluorescence value relative to the blank and the other proteins, which proved that AMP-CDs@Tb had a better anti-interference ability in detecting BSA, and it was not affected by the other proteins, as shown in Fig. 5 (B). In addition, the interference experiment of 10 mM common anions and cations (NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−, Ag+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+) in blood on BSA detection was selected, and the results were shown in Fig. S7, it was seen that the metal ions had little effect on the detection of BSA. These effects were almost negligible. The contents of Na+, Cl−, K+, and Ca2+ in the blood were 135, 95, 3.5, and 2.0 mM in bovine serum. When the blood was diluted 100 times for the experiment, the concentrations of Na+, Cl−, K+, and Ca2+ in the blood were only 1.35, 0.95, 0.035, and 0.02 mM. When the anion and cation anti-interference experiment was done, the concentration added was 10 mM, which was much higher than the content in the actual sample. Therefore, the influence of anions and cations in the 100-fold diluted blood sample could be ignored.

According to the fluorescence spectra of AMP-CDs@Tb with different concentrations of BSA added in Fig. S8. There was good linear relationship between the concentration of BSA and the fluorescence signal of AMP-CDs@Tb. The linear regression equations were y = 0.154x + 2.419 (R2 = 0.994) and y = 0.027x + 4.839 (R2 = 0.998), BSA concentration ranges were 0.1 to 20 μM and 50 to 650 μM, respectively (Fig. 5 (C)). Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.042 μM based on the following equation LOD = 3σ/k, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank sample and k was the slope of the calibration curve. This value was much lower than the limit concentration of BSA in bovine serum. Comparisons with other literature reports were listed in Table S1 in the Supporting Information.

Different batches of bovine serum were diluted 100 times with Tris-HCl as blank groups. Different concentrations of BSA were added to the blank groups, and the BSA content was detected by AMP-CDs@Tb. As shown in Tables 1–3
, the recovery rates of BSA in the spiked samples of the three bovine sera were 87.80–115.10 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 0.89 % (n = 3). The results showed that the AMP-CDs@Tb fluorescent probe based on the ratio fluorescence method could meet the requirement of the detection of BSA in bovine serum.

To further verify the accuracy and feasibility of the developed method, the currently common UV method for detecting BSA was selected as a comparison [60–62]. Due to the characteristic absorption peak of BSA at 285 nm, different concentrations of BSA were chosen to plot the standard curve and the results showed good linearity from 1 to 50 μM shown in Fig. S9 of the supporting information. The results of the spiked recovery experiments for both methods were analyzed using the f-test and t-test in the supporting information.

As shown in Fig. 6 (A), the average lifetime of AMP-CDs@Tb was 6.1 ns, including the τ1 component (3.1 ns) and the τ2 component (8.0 ns), with proportions of 38.7 % and 61.3 %, respectively. The average lifetime of AMP-CDs@Tb-BSA was 5.6 ns, including the τ1 component (2.6 ns) and the τ2 component (7.6 ns), with proportions of 39.7 % and 60.3 %, respectively. Proportions were 39.7 % and 60.3 %, respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes all remained unchanged after the addition of BSA, indicating that the fluorescence burst of AMP-CDs@Tb could be attributed to the formation of non-luminous ground-state complexes, i.e., the static burst mechanism. The possible burst mechanism was illustrated in Fig. 6 (B), AMP achieved different functions through its rich functional groups. Herein an AMP served as both a precursor of CDs and a ligand for binding to Tb3+. This dual functionality was attributed to the molecular structure of AMP and its chemical behavior under different reaction conditions.

AMP was both a precursor of CDs and a ligand of Tb3+, the essential factor resided in the control of the reaction stages. On the one hand, AMP contained adenine (A), ribose and phosphate groups. CDs with fluorescent property were prepared by hydrothermal method under high temperature or high-pressure conditions. In this process, the organic components in AMP underwent dehydration condensation reaction under high temperature or high pressure to form a nanostructure with carbon as the core and various functional groups (such as phosphate, carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl) on the surface. The ribose and adenine parts in AMP were carbonized during heat treatment to form small carbon-based nanoparticles, while the phosphate group part was partially retained or participated in surface functionalization, providing additional active sites. On the other hand, the AMP provided CDs rich surface functional groups, especially phosphate (PO₄3−) and nitrogen atoms on adenine. PO₄3− exhibited a strong negative charge and coordinated with positively charged Tb3+ to form a bond. This was one of the main mechanisms of AMP as a ligand of Tb3+. At the same time, the adenine part of AMP contained basic nitrogen atoms, which was able to also coordinate with Tb3+ to form a more complex coordination environment, making the metal complex more stable. These functional groups served as ligands to form stable coordination compounds with rare earth metal ions (Tb3+). After CDs was generated, Tb3+ ions coordinate with the phosphate groups or amino groups remaining on the surface of CDs. Since AMP originally carried these coordination functional groups, the surface of CDs was functionalized to enabled it to coordinate with Tb3+ to form a stable Tb3+-AMP composite material.

Since Tb3+ had good fluorescence properties, and CDs usually had unique luminescence properties. The dual role of AMP as a precursor and ligand of CDs could produce a synergistic effect, which made the composite material produce fluorescent dual emission characteristics. As a protein, BSA had multiple functional sites that can interact with CDs or metal ions, such as amino, carboxyl and thiol groups. The interaction of these functional groups with CDs or Tb3+ could change the fluorescence of the composite material. The carboxyl or amino groups in BSA could coordinate with Tb3+, resulted in a change in the coordination environment. This coordination effected might affect the fluorescence emission efficiency of Tb3+. Generally, the introduction of BSA could affect energy transfer and enhance or quench the fluorescence emission of Tb3+. The surface groups of BSA could also interact with AMP CDs, such as binding to CDs through electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bonding, resulted in changes in the fluorescence of CDs.

This was followed by further discussion of the relationship between AMP-CDs@Tb and BSA, a globular heart-shaped protein consisting of 583 amino acid residues with a molecular weight of approximately 66.4 KDa, as shown in Fig. 6 (C). It contained three homology domains (I, II, and III), which were subdivided into 9 loops (L1–L9) connected by 17 disulfide bonds, and the loops in each homology domain composed of large L1–L9, and the ring in each homology domain consisted of three sequence rings, small-large. Each structural domain contained two subdomains (IA, IB, etc.) X-crystal diffraction data showed that the structure of albumin was predominantly α-helical, with the rest of the polypeptide forming random convolutions, and there was no β-folding between malleable or flexible regions of the sub-structural domains. The surface of BSA contained many acidic and basic amino acids. Among them, there were 40 and 59 acidic amino acids glutamic acid and aspartic acid, respectively, and 59 and 23 basic amino acids lysine and arginine, respectively [63]. Among these, aspartic acid (the most abundant acidic amino acid) and lysine (a basic amino acid) were selected for DFT investigate the relationship between the structure of the CDs and their optical properties. (The structures were optimized by B3LYP-D3 generalized function theory using the 6-31G (d, p) basis set. (All calculations were performed using the Gaussian 16 program package)). Generally, the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) was related to the absorption and emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 6 (D), the optimized structure of AMP-CDs had a HOMO of −4.092 eV, a LUMO of −2.614 eV, and an energy band gap of 1.478 eV. Based on the HOMO and the LUMO of the AMP-CDs obtained from the DFT calculations, it was observed that the electron charges were randomly distributed on the AMP-CDs. Furthermore, by comparing the LUMO and HOMO of AMP-CDs, it was concluded that both LUMO and HOMO were dominated by the framework of AMP-CDs, which suggested that electrons in the HOMO could be excited to the LUMO with sufficient energy. It was hypothesized that the carboxyl group of aspartic acid in BSA bound to the amino group of AMP with a HOMO of −4.072 eV, a LUMO of −2.944 eV, and an energy band gap of 1.128 eV. The amino group of lysine in BSA was bound to the carboxyl group of AMP with a HOMO of −2.699 eV, a LUMO of −2.060 eV, and an energy band gap of 0.639 eV. Compared to the energy band gap of AMP-CDs, there was a decrease in both acidic and basic amino acid binding to BSA. The lower the energy band gap, the more energy was required to reach the activated state, then the reaction was difficult to occur, and the chemical properties of the substance were more stable. Therefore, it was proved that AMP-CDs could bound to BSA.

Fig. 6 (E) showed the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the prepared AMP-CDs. The UV absorption spectrum (red line), fluorescence excitation spectrum (black line), and emission spectrum (blue line) of AMP-CDs was measured in the figure. From the figure, obvious UV absorption peaks were observed, in which the absorption peak at 295 nm was the π-π* leap of CC due to the sp2 hybridization of AMP-CDs, and the absorption peak at 360 nm corresponded to the n-π* leap of CO and CN. From the excitation spectra and emission spectra, the blue fluorescence emission peak of AMP-CDs was located at 420 nm, and by fixing this emission wavelength, the maximum excitation wavelength of the CDs peak was 360 nm. The UV absorption peak of AMP-CDs at 360 nm overlapped with the characteristic peak at 360 nm in the excitation spectrum, which indicated that AMP-CDs hardly underwent non-radiative leaps. Non-radiative jumps occurred. The UV absorption spectrum (red line), fluorescence excitation spectrum (black line), and emission spectrum (green line at 425 nm, blue line at 545 nm) of AMP-CDs@Tb was measured as shown in Fig. 6 (F). After doping with Tb ions, its peak at UV–visible 295 nm was changed, and the functional groups of AMP-CDs was not greatly altered. There were five distinct peaks in the emission spectrum, including the emission peak of AMP-CDs at 425 nm, and the characteristic peaks of Tb ions at 490 nm (5D4 → 7F6), 545 nm (5D4 → 7F5), and 625 nm (5D4 → 7F3). The multiplicative peak was at 580 nm. As shown by the UV–vis spectrum of Fig. 6 (G), the inset was a local zoom in at 280–320 nm, the absorption peaks of the AMP-CDs was red-shifted with the addition of Tb3+ and the absorbance was enhanced, which further suggested that there was an interaction between the material and Tb3+, and the AMP-CDs@Tb was successfully synthesized.

In this paper, a single-excited double-emitting probe AMP-CDs@Tb was successfully synthesized with high anti-interference ability and high sensitivity and used as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor to realize rapid and sensitive analysis of bovine serum albumin (BSA). The morphology and functional groups of the fabricated double-emitting CDs was analyzed by a series of characterization methods, which indicated that the lattice spacing of AMP-CDs@Tb, and there were both sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbons with a certain graphitized structure. The BSA was detected by AMP-CDs@Tb, and the ratio of 420 and 545 nm fluorescence intensities F420/F545 in the emission spectra was increased with the increase of the concentration of BSA and showed good linear relationship when concentration of BSA were 0.1 to 20 μM and 50 to 650 μM. The limit of detection of BSA was calculated to be 0.042 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The recoveries of BSA in bovine serum spiked samples were in the range of 87.80–115.10 % with the relative standard deviations of less than 0.89 % (n = 3), and the fluorescent probe satisfied the detection of BSA in bovine serum. The AMP-CDs@Tb dual-emission fluorescent probe achieved rapid and sensitive detection of BSA with high anti-interference ability and high sensitivity, provided a new idea for fluorescent probe detection of proteins, and would have wide application in the field of bioanalysis.

Yuwei Guan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xue Shuai: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiangyan Ruan: Writing – review & editing. Yuejiao Wang: Data curation. Yun Wei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
This article containing the study with animal materials was approved by the ethical committee of Beijing University of Chemical Technology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 22074004) and Beijing Nova Program (No. 20230484438).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.139049.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of carbon dot nanozymes for ratiometric dual-signal and smartphone-assisted visual detection of nitrite in food matrices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Hao, Z. Wang, H. Wang, W. Dong, Y. Liu, Q. Hu, et al., Rational design of carbon dot nanozymes for ratiometric dual-signal and smartphone-assisted visual detection of nitrite in food matrices, J Hazard Mater, 2024, 480, 136156.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular imprinting-based triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor with aggregation-induced emission effect for visual detection of doxycycline

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Miao, J. Yu, X. Zhao, X. Chen, C. Zhu, X. Cao, et al., Molecular imprinting-based triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor with aggregation-induced emission effect for visual detection of doxycycline, J Hazard Mater, 2024, 470, 134218.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectroscopic determination of tryptophan and tyrosine in proteins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Edelhoch, Spectroscopic determination of tryptophan and tyrosine in proteins, Biochemistry, 1967, 6(7), 1948-1954.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calculation of protein extinction coefficients from amino acid sequence data

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.C.G.a.P.H.v. Hippel, Calculation of protein extinction coefficients from amino acid sequence data, Anal Biochem, 1989, 182(2), 319-326.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric and turbidimetric methods for measuring proteins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. LAYNE, Spectrophotometric and turbidimetric methods for measuring proteins, Method Enzymol, 1957, 3, 447-454.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Applications of human and bovine serum albumins in biomedical engineering: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, J. Hu, H. Xue, Y. Hu, Y.N. Liu, G. Lin, et al., Applications of human and bovine serum albumins in biomedical engineering: A review, Int J Biol Macromol, 2023, 253, 126914.
